item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept would probably be a no vote from me. But it's written well enough to make me interested and care about the character so +1.

Also congrats on 300!

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: For reference, the source for this image is here, and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was rather interesting to me. Rather well written and explained.
Personally, it deserves the rating it has. A +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good storytelling within. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this not have the "military" tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Based on one of the tales involving him, he's extremely skilled with melee weapons, so he could be an excellent trainer for agents. He'd need a translator, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good concept, and nicely executed. Quite sympathetic too.

Upvoted.

I'd like to see a few more interview logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no evidence that other Roman dead were not ALSO cursed to remain trapped in their corpses - this is just the only one that has been found and found in a way that allowed it to escape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty extreme powers to be had by Jugurtha. It's most likely unnecessary and just my own interest in ancient history, but I feel like maybe Jugurtha would have also cursed Bocchus and/or Sulla, though they died decades later, and perhaps some reference to it, or as to why he didn't curse them too could be addressed. Or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Various soulbound items are far older than 963, and indeed from the site itself. Items such as this are the stuff of myths. Now, if you think it's derivative, that's your right, but at least compare it to the stuff it was actually based on to some capacity.

E: And that without even mentioning the difference in effects: 963's effect are permanent, 1510 are temporary. 963's effects override the existing personality entirely, rather than the blanketing effect 1510 has. 963 will work on anything, 1510 just on very specific people, and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhhhhhh….what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Umm, what?

How is this in any way similar to SCP-963? The latter introduced a key character to site lore, and Bright can hop from body to body. This one is a guy trapped in a Roman helmet.

What made you draw the comparison?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote.
Seriously, this is just 963 only with another person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got here through 361, love them both. Excellent work! A tale in SCP form, but it flows very naturally. I kind of like the ancient curse thing. It adds this whole myery to it as to why the curse worked.
Romans. Romans? Romans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want some more stuff with 1510, you can check Djoric's Anachronisms and SCP-361.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really like to see some tales for this. this is a really good scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a bit of the Helmet of Dr. Fate, but pretty cool. I got linked here by your newest article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'd be cursing, too, if i found an unfaithful woman on a tablet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't have thought much of the concept, but you've done a good job with it. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do rather like this. Horrifying yet awesome concept. I would so like to see him put to use by a junior researcher during a containment breach. Or him teaching old skills to new Agents. Legionaries were tough and well trained soldiers after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been considering writing a tale about it, though what I really would like is some collaboration with Lord Blackwood (which I adore). Maybe I should talk to Djoric about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, and whats there is wonderful. it just feels like it is.. lacking quantity. perhaps collaborate with other SCP makers to extend the story?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I shouldn't like this, but I do. I want to see some of the little things ironed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig the interviews and the backstory for this. I will say that "found his death" breaks tone. "Died" is fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, this is the abridged version. The actual thing was twice as long, and had pretty much every part of the body on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those Romans sure liked their curses specific, didn't they?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this fella; I'd especially love some sort of follow-up, showing several things he said he'd seen in his travels.

"Vases which spew godly fire upon command of heathen armies."
Oil-based flame weapons from the Orient, by way of Arabian traders, with the fuel kept in earthenware containers. No anomalies.

"Great statues which come alive play their trumpets upon the arrival of dignitaries."
Ancient mechanism-driven devices portrayed to the masses as 'miraculous.' No anomalies.

"Boats which sail upon the land as swiftly as the sea."
Archeological remains of an apparent shipyard constructed [blank] miles inland; depictions of standard sailing ships gliding across solid ground. No remains of actual ships found.

[DATA EXPUNGED]
[DATA EXPUNGED], transferred to Sector 32, Biological Hazards wing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely so, in fact. The curse is a revised version of a real Roman curse meant for an unfaithful woman found on a tablet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From the POV of the subject, it is very much a contemporary curse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, I'll keep the tagging in mind next time. The entire "offer insight about SCP" isn't really the focus- he has no idea how to contain anything, he just tells old war stories which the Foundation happens to find useful on occasion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm honestly not sure how this has gotten so highly rated. It's a personality that takes over a human subject, and offers insight into other SCPs and tells us how they might be contained. In my experience that's been an automatic downvote…

Also, if you can't think of any attribute tags, please leave it for someone else. :3 Just adding 'safe' and 'scp' removes it from the filters of people who watch for untagged pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed the tonal issues, but at the same time I liked his speech- quite a few bits of it are altered from genuine period text (the curse, for example, is an altered curse tablet meant for a woman). I'm not sure it would feel the same if he talk plainly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone's a little off in a few places — nothing major, but enou to bother me. Also, Aetius talks like a poet, not a soldier; I have a hard time believing that a Roman legionnaire would use such flowery diction.

Nevertheless, I do like the idea. No vote because of the tone issues, but I'd be happy to help clean those up if you like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ancient Roman Bright? Or, you know, BOOM!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect this one would take precedence, but the host would still be Bright once the helmet is removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could upvote this comment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what happens if we put 963 and this on one person?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Veni, vidi, upvoti.
:3
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like "ancient curses" as a basis for SCPs - too Stephen King.

Aside from that, I like the helmet and the man.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really great, I love his diction as he describes his experiences. Would enjoy seeing it developed since his current existence seems pretty open to more happening with him, whether dark/horrible or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for anything Roman, but this one holds merit on its own. I'm actually starting to feel sympathetic for this guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would replace "him" with "the subject".

otherwise interesting, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a few spelling/grammatical errors for you. Overall I like the idea and the execution. With a little bit of cleaning up, it could be even better. +1
